title: Quantitative Detection of Neuronal Na+ Transients Using Multi-photon Excitation in Mouse Brain Slices
author: Sara Eitelmann
license_note: PMC full-text XML — Bio-protocol — CC-BY confirmed in article XML

steps.title: Standard ACSF Reagent Final concentration in...
steps.instruction: Standard ACSF Reagent Final concentration in double-distilled water NaCl 130 mM KCl 2.5 mM CaCl 2 2 mM MgCl 2 1 mM NaH 2 PO 4 1.25 mM NaHCO 3 26 mM Glucose 10 mM Adjust pH to 7.4 by bubbling with carbogen (95% O 2 and 5% CO 2 ). The recommended overall volume is 1,000 mL.

steps.title: Preparation ACSF Reagent Final concentration in...
steps.instruction: Preparation ACSF Reagent Final concentration in double-distilled water NaCl 130 mM KCl 2.5 mM CaCl 2 0.5 mM MgCl 2 6 mM NaH 2 PO 4 1.25 mM NaHCO 3 26 mM Glucose 10 mM Adjust pH to 7.4 by bubbling with carbogen (95% O 2 and 5% CO 2 ). The recommended overall volume is 1,000 mL.

steps.title: HEPES-based ACSF Reagent Final concentration in...
steps.instruction: HEPES-based ACSF Reagent Final concentration in double-distilled water NaCl 125 mM KCl 3 mM CaCl 2 2 mM MgSO 4 2 mM NaH 2 PO 4 1.25 mM HEPES 25 mM Glucose 10 mM Adjust pH to 7.4 by titration with NaOH. The recommended overall volume is 100 mL.

steps.title: ACSF for calibration Note
steps.instruction: ACSF for calibration Note: Prepare calibration solutions with different sodium concentrations (e.g., 0, 10, 50, and 100 mM) by adjusting the concentration of K + , maintaining osmolarity. Solutions need to be prepared freshly every day. Reagent Final concentration in double-distilled water Na + + K + 150 mM Cl- 30 mM Gluconic acid 120 mM HEPES 10 mM MgSO 4 2 mM CaCl 2 2 mM Gramicidin 3 μM Monensin 10 μM Ouabain 1 mM Adjust pH to 7.4 by titration with KOH. The recommended overall volume is 500 mL for each.

steps.instruction: SR101 stock solution Reagent Final concentration Quantity or Volume SR101 1.5 mM - Double-distilled H 2 O - 1 mL

steps.instruction: ING-2 AM stock solution Reagent Final concentration Quantity or Volume ING-2 AM 1 mM - Pluronic 20 mg/mL - DMSO - 46.12 μL

steps.title: Bolus-loading solution Reagent Final concentration Quantity...
steps.instruction: Bolus-loading solution Reagent Final concentration Quantity or Volume ING-2 AM stock solution 125 μM 2 μL HEPES-based ACSF - 14 μL

steps.title: IRF Solution Note
steps.instruction: IRF Solution Note: Dissolve potassium iodide in H 2 O to saturation. Then, add 0.5 mg of Erythrosin B per 5 mL of H 2 O. Reagent Final concentration Quantity or Volume Potassium iodide Until saturation - Double-distilled H 2 O - 5 mL Erythrosin B - 0.5 mg Laboratory supplies

steps.title: To obtain acute brain slices, mice...
steps.instruction: To obtain acute brain slices, mice older than postnatal day 14 are anesthetized with CO 2 and subsequently rapidly euthanized by decapitation (for a detailed explanation of animal policies, see also the section Ethical considerations) ( Figure 2 , step 1). The procedure can also be used for adult animals or animals younger than P

steps.title: Note, however, that different protocols for anesthesia may apply
steps.instruction: Note, however, that different protocols for anesthesia may apply.

steps.title: Following decapitation, remove the brain rapidly...
steps.instruction: Following decapitation, remove the brain rapidly and place it in a Petri dish containing ice-cold preparation ACSF. To maintain a pH of 7.4, ACSF must be continuously bubbled with carbogen (95% O 2 and 5% CO 2 ) ( Figure 2 , step 2).

steps.title: After the separation of the hemispheres,...
steps.instruction: After the separation of the hemispheres, make an additional incision in a parasagittal orientation (approximately 1.5 mm thick). Affix the tissue block containing the hippocampus with glue to the cutting stage of the microtome at the parasagittal incision. Place the cutting stage into the microtome and cool (ideally with a non-dissolving cooling element) ( Figure 2 , step 3).

steps.title: Prepare hippocampal slices with a thickness...
steps.instruction: Prepare hippocampal slices with a thickness of 250 μm, keeping the tissue constantly submerged in ice-cold preparation ACSF ( Figure 2 , step 4).

steps.title: Transfer the slices very gently to...
steps.instruction: Transfer the slices very gently to ACSF containing 1 μM SR101 at 34 °C for 20 min, during which they will take up the astrocyte marker. Following this, incubate the slices at 34 °C for a further 10 min in standard ACSF. Slices can be used for experiments for approximately 5–7 h if kept at room temperature ( Figure 2 , step 5). Figure

steps.title: Protocol for acute brain slice preparation
steps.instruction: Protocol for acute brain slice preparation.

steps.title: Brain isolation starts by cutting along the illustrated dashed lines
steps.instruction: Brain isolation starts by cutting along the illustrated dashed lines. Note: It is necessary to work fast to minimize the time period in which the brain is not submerged in ice-cold ACSF (see below). Always use maximum care to prevent damage to the tissue probes.

steps.title: Excess tissue is removed with additional...
steps.instruction: Excess tissue is removed with additional trimming cuts as depicted by dashed lines.

steps.title: Parasagittal trimming is achieved by cutting...
steps.instruction: Parasagittal trimming is achieved by cutting a single hemisphere at a 45° angle.

steps.title: The hemisphere is glued to the...
steps.instruction: The hemisphere is glued to the vibratome cutting stage and placed in the buffer tray filled with ice-cold, carbonated modified ACSF.

steps.title: Freshly cut slices are placed onto...
steps.instruction: Freshly cut slices are placed onto a mesh positioned in a beaker filled with modified, ice-cold ACSF.

steps.title: Just before starting experiments, slices are...
steps.instruction: Just before starting experiments, slices are positioned in the experimental chamber and secured with a fine grid.

steps.title: Bolus-loading of hippocampal CA1 region
steps.instruction: Bolus-loading of hippocampal CA1 region. B. Bolus-loading of ING-2 into hippocampal brain slices Note: This technique was initially developed for in vivo loading of dyes into brain cells [16]. It has since been adapted for use with acute brain slices and is employed by numerous research laboratories, including our own.

steps.title: Prepare pipettes for dye injection (tip...
steps.instruction: Prepare pipettes for dye injection (tip diameter of approximately 1 μm and a resistance of 1–3 MΩ) from fire-polished borosilicate glass capillaries using a standard micropipette puller.

steps.title: Place a hippocampal brain slice into...
steps.instruction: Place a hippocampal brain slice into a microscope chamber and fix it with a “harp” (e.g., ALA Scientific HSG-5A) ( Figure 2 , step 6). After placing the chamber in your imaging system, constantly perfuse the brain slice with standard ACSF (typical perfusion rate is 2 mL/min).

steps.title: Load an injection pipette with bolus-loading...
steps.instruction: Load an injection pipette with bolus-loading solution (see Recipes) and attach the pipette to the pressure application device.

steps.title: Position the pipette with care and...
steps.instruction: Position the pipette with care and the help of a micromanipulator into your region of choice in the tissue ( Figure 2 , step 7). Dye injection should be close to the cellular region you are interested in, usually in the same field of view. For loading of somata of neurons in the CA1 pyramidal area, the dye injection pipette should be positioned, e.g., in the stratum radiatum, just below the CA1 pyramidal layer ( Figure 3 , step 1). Apply ~1.5 psi for ~5 s to inject the dye into the tissue. This process should be repeated until the entire field of view is covered ( Figure 3 , step 1).

steps.title: Wait for 30–45 min to engage...
steps.instruction: Wait for 30–45 min to engage intracellular de-esterification and wash off excess dye from the extracellular space, constantly perfusing the brain slice with standard ACSF. Figure

steps.title: Calibration process
steps.instruction: Calibration process.

steps.title: Inject ING-2 at the region of interest
steps.instruction: Inject ING-2 at the region of interest. Here, a fluorescence lifetime image of the CA1 region of a hippocampal tissue slice is shown. Pipettes and approximate areas, dye-loaded by one injection, are indicated in white.

steps.title: Perfuse the slice with Na +...
steps.instruction: Perfuse the slice with Na + -free saline to wash out extracellular Na + .

steps.title: Perfuse with 0 Na + calibration...
steps.instruction: Perfuse with 0 Na + calibration solution containing ionophores to equilibrate intra- and extracellular Na + .

steps.title: Continue perfusion with calibration solutions at different Na + concentrations
steps.instruction: Continue perfusion with calibration solutions at different Na + concentrations.

steps.title: Plot the resulting lifetimes against Na...
steps.instruction: Plot the resulting lifetimes against Na + using appropriate software (e.g., Origin Pro).

steps.title: Fit the resulting data to determine...
steps.instruction: Fit the resulting data to determine the apparent K D(app) and dynamic range of the dye. Modified and taken from [3]. C. Fluorescence lifetime imaging microscopy of ING-2 and multi-photon excitation

steps.title: Choose suitable parameters for imaging for...
steps.instruction: Choose suitable parameters for imaging for your given imaging system and the specific dye used. With multiphoton excitation, ING-2 can be excited over a broad wavelength spectrum from 740 to 1,100 nm. Typical parameters for ING-2 used in our experiments are an excitation wavelength of 840 nm, an image size of 512 × 512 pixels, and an imaging frequency of 1 Hz. Note

steps.title: The whole setup should be tuned for maximum photon efficacy
steps.instruction: The whole setup should be tuned for maximum photon efficacy. The more photons that can be recorded, the better the spatio-temporal resolution will be. Temporal resolution is always relative, as the recorded frames can be binned afterward. However, a minimum number of photons (for Na + -sensitive dyes 15–20 photons per pixel and frame) need to be recorded for proper lifetime calculation. Note

steps.title: The setup process before every experiment...
steps.instruction: The setup process before every experiment is crucial to obtain high-quality results. Make sure that the laser power under the objective does not exceed 5 mW. Use appropriate emission filters and dichroic mirrors to ensure optimal detection conditions. For ING-2, emission can be collected above 560 nm wavelength.

steps.title: Measure the instrument response function (IRF)...
steps.instruction: Measure the instrument response function (IRF) of your microscope with the IRF solution (see Recipes) using comparable photon counts as during experimental conditions (e.g., same laser power).

steps.title: Select your region of interest in your tissue preparation
steps.instruction: Select your region of interest in your tissue preparation. Critical: Make sure that your tissue sample is healthy and that many viable neurons are present. Injured neurons are usually characterized by their swollen cell bodies. Moreover, dendrites may be distorted and not be clearly visible.

steps.title: Record an image (e
steps.instruction: Record an image (e.g., an average of 30 frames) in standard ACSF to check initial conditions.

steps.title: Start a time series in standard...
steps.instruction: Start a time series in standard ACSF and record fluorescence lifetime in baseline conditions (e.g., for 30 s).

steps.title: Next, record a time series performing...
steps.instruction: Next, record a time series performing your manipulation of choice to test if this results in a change in fluorescence lifetimes (and intracellular Na + ). As a positive control, bath application of glutamate (e.g., 1 mM glutamate for 10 s) causes reliable and prominent intracellular Na + signals in rodent hippocampal slices [17]. Moreover, bath application of inhibitors of cellular ATP production induces long-lasting and large increases in intracellular Na + in the same preparation [2,3,18]. Alternatively, substances can be focally applied to a soma or a dendrite. This is usually done with a fine glass pipette (comparable to the ones used in section B for bolus application but with a smaller tip diameter) that is filled with the substance of choice (e.g., 1 mM glutamate). The application duration can be a lot shorter in comparison to bath applications and is usually below 1 s [3,19,20].

steps.title: Check if the changes induced by...
steps.instruction: Check if the changes induced by the manipulation are reversible, i.e., if fluorescence lifetimes recover to the initial baseline levels. After the lifetimes have returned to initial values for at least 30 frames, stop the time series.

steps.title: Take another image (e
steps.instruction: Take another image (e.g., again an average of 30 frames) to compare starting and finishing conditions in standard ACSF. D. Calibrating changes in lifetime to changes in Na + Note: The general calibration routine for Na + -sensitive indicator dyes was described in detail by Rose and Ransom [21] for cultured astrocytes. It was later adapted for acute hippocampal brain slices and widefield and confocal as well as multi-photon microscopy [3,22,23]. This protocol was first used for FLIM by Meyer et al. [2].

steps.title: Prepare calibration solutions with different Na...
steps.instruction: Prepare calibration solutions with different Na + concentrations, ranging from unsaturated (0 mM Na + ) to saturated (>100 mM Na + ) concentrations. Caution: Select a frame rate and laser power, which enables the collection of sufficient photons under all conditions.

steps.title: Start perfusing a dye-loaded tissue slice ( Figure 3 ,
steps.instruction: Start perfusing a dye-loaded tissue slice ( Figure 3 ,

steps.title: with standard ACSF and then switch...
steps.instruction: with standard ACSF and then switch to 0 mM Na + solution without the ionophores and ouabain to wash out Na + from the tissue ( Figure 3 , step 2).

steps.title: Record the resulting changes in fluorescence lifetime
steps.instruction: Record the resulting changes in fluorescence lifetime.

steps.title: Then, perfuse the slice with 0...
steps.instruction: Then, perfuse the slice with 0 mM Na + solution containing the ionophores and ouabain to promote washout of Na + from the cells and to enable equilibration of Na + between intra- and extracellular space ( Figure 3 , step 3).

steps.title: Switch to calibration salines containing different Na + concentrations (e
steps.instruction: Switch to calibration salines containing different Na + concentrations (e.g., 10, 20, 30, 50, and 100 mM), again recording changes in fluorescence lifetime resulting from the changes in Na + ( Figure 3 , step 4). Note: Always wait before switching to the next concentration until a stable plateau has been reached. You might need to adjust the focus of your microscope during the calibration process.

steps.title: Plot the changes in lifetime against...
steps.instruction: Plot the changes in lifetime against Na + and use a Michaelis–Menten equation to fit the data points and to determine the apparent K d and the dynamic range/saturation of the dye used ( Figure 3 , step 5).

steps.title: The resulting fit will also allow...
steps.instruction: The resulting fit will also allow you to directly convert fluorescence lifetime values obtained during different experiments into Na + concentrations ( Figure 3 , step 6).

description: Validation of protocol This protocol or parts of it has been used and validated in the following research articles: • Meyer et al. [2]. Quantitative Determination of Cellular [Na + ] By Fluorescence Lifetime Imaging With CoroNaGreen. Journal of General Physiology . • Meyer et al. [3]. Rapid Fluorescence Lifetime Imaging Reveals That TRPV4 Channels Promote Dysregulation of Neuronal Na + in Ischemia. Journal of Neuroscience . These two publications illustrate and describe the above-mentioned in situ calibrations in detail, also providing approaches for dye calibration in vitro. Meyer et al. [3] described rapidFLIM in rodent brain tissue slices, also demonstrating that this technique enables reliable, unbiased determination of baseline Na + concentrations in CA1 pyramidal neurons. Moreover, Meyer et al. [3] presented different manipulations to induce reversible changes in neuronal Na + and their recording by rapidFLIM. Figure 4 shows that inhibition of cellular metabolism by perfusing tissue slices with glucose-free saline containing inhibitors of glycolysis and oxidative phosphorylation results in large increases in Na + in CA1 pyramidal neurons. Figure 4. Quantitative, dynamic measurement of Na + loading in hippocampal neurons using multi-photon excitation and rapid FLIM with ING-2. A. Color-coded fluorescence lifetime images (30 s temporal binning) of the intracellular Na + depicting the CA1 pyramidal cell layer of an ING-2-loaded acute hippocampal tissue slice. Right: color code. Top left: image showing intracellular Na + at baseline conditions; middle: change in Na + upon mild chemical ischemia (2 min of perfusion with 2 mM NaN 3 and 5 mM 2-Desoxyglucose); right: recovery taken ~20 min after starting perfusion with inhibitors. Bottom: similar illustration, demonstrating the neuronal Na + loading upon moderate chemical ischemia (same solution as before but with 5 min of application). B. Changes in intracellular Na + induced by mild (2 min; left) and moderate (5 min
